Experiment_description: A preliminary baseline study investigating 'confidence drift' — the within-conversation variance of verbalized confidence scores — as a hallucination detection signal in multi-turn LLM conversations. All nodes use synthetic QA datasets (no actual LLMs) where hallucinated answers are modeled with higher confidence variance across turns than correct answers. Multiple hallucination detection signals are compared: (1) single-turn confidence (baseline), (2) drift variance (within-conversation confidence variance), and (3) Drift-Augmented Calibration (DAC) combining both signals via hand-weighting, logistic regression, or a small neural network. Nodes differ in their synthetic data generation complexity (simple Gaussian noise vs. noise plus sycophantic pressure) and in how they implement DAC fusion (hand-combined, logistic regression, or neural network).
Significance: Hallucination detection in LLMs is a critical safety problem. Most existing approaches rely on single-turn confidence estimates, which are known to be unreliable. This study tests the hypothesis that temporal inconsistency in expressed confidence across conversation turns provides a stronger hallucination signal than any single-turn snapshot. Validating this hypothesis — even on synthetic data — motivates the design of multi-turn elicitation protocols for hallucination detection. The finding that drift variance substantially outperforms single-turn confidence (by 0.15–0.19 AUROC points) across multiple independent implementations, and that 4–5 turns are sufficient to capture most of the signal, provides concrete experimental guidance for future real-LLM studies. The comparison of DAC fusion methods (hand-combined, logistic regression, neural) also reveals that simple linear fusion can match or marginally exceed drift variance alone, while complex models can hurt performance on near-linearly-separable feature spaces.
Description: Each node generates a synthetic multi-turn QA dataset where each conversation contains multiple turns asking about the same factual claim. Hallucinated answers are assigned higher Gaussian noise variance in their turn-by-turn confidence scores (some nodes also add a sycophantic pressure component that shifts confidence after challenging turns). For each claim, the within-conversation variance of confidence scores is computed as the 'drift' signal. Three detection methods are compared: (1) Single-turn confidence: the confidence score from the first turn only; (2) Drift variance: the variance of confidence scores across all turns; (3) DAC (Drift-Augmented Calibration): a combination of mean confidence and drift variance, implemented as either a hand-weighted linear combination (Node 1ca0), a logistic regression meta-classifier (Nodes afabe/7401/153), or a small neural network (Nodes afabe/7401/153). Detection performance is evaluated via AUROC on held-out test sets, and sensitivity to the number of conversation turns is analyzed. Confidence trajectories and score distributions are visualized to qualitatively confirm the drift phenomenon. Nodes afabe14, 7401bb, and 153b86 produced identical numerical results, indicating they shared the same implementation. Node 1ca0a07 used a simpler data generation model and hand-combined DAC. Node eba798 used a more sophisticated simulator with sycophantic pressure.

List_of_included_plots.description: The bar chart confirms the aggregate performance across all turns: Drift Variance achieves AUROC 0.9399, DAC 0.8316, Single-Turn Confidence 0.7466. The gap between drift and single-turn (~0.19 AUROC) is large, supporting the central hypothesis that temporal inconsistency is a strong hallucination signal. However, DAC underperforms drift alone by ~0.108 AUROC, which is counter to the abstract's claim that DAC improves over baselines; in this synthetic experiment, the best baseline is already drift variance, and the combination hurts. This points to either (i) DAC is not tuned (e.g., linear combination weights, normalization) or (ii) the synthetic data generation makes drift dominate so strongly that adding single-turn confidence dilutes separability.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot from Node 1ca0 (simpler data generation, hand-combined DAC) shows the clearest case where combining signals hurts: hand-weighted DAC (0.8316) falls between single-turn (0.7466) and drift variance (0.9399), demonstrating that naive fusion without learned weights can dilute a strong signal. The ~0.19 AUROC gap between drift and single-turn is the key empirical support for the confidence drift hypothesis in this simpler setting.

List_of_included_plots.description: Hallucination detection AUROC comparison (test, Synthetic QA): Single-turn confidence achieves AUROC 0.849, whereas drift variance alone reaches 0.994. DAC (LogReg combining features) reaches 0.998, a small but consistent gain over drift alone, implying single-turn confidence adds marginal complementary information beyond drift. Neural DAC is lower at 0.951—substantially worse than the simple drift statistic and logistic DAC—suggesting optimization/architecture mismatch, insufficient data, regularization issues, or that the neural model is over-parameterized for a near-linearly separable feature space.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot from the more sophisticated simulation (Nodes afabe/7401/153) shows that logistic regression DAC (0.998) marginally outperforms drift variance alone (0.994), while neural DAC (0.951) underperforms both. This directly contrasts with Node 1ca0's hand-combined DAC which hurt performance. The key insight is that the fusion method matters: learned linear fusion (logistic regression) can preserve the drift signal while adding marginal benefit from single-turn confidence, whereas both hand-weighting and neural networks can degrade performance.

List_of_included_plots.description: AUROC vs conversation length indicates drift-based detection benefits from additional turns. Drift Variance (blue) rises from ~0.80 at 2 turns to ~0.91 at 3 turns and then saturates around ~0.94 from 4–8 turns, consistent with variance estimation improving with more samples and then hitting a ceiling. Single-turn confidence (red) is flat at ~0.747 regardless of length, as expected since it does not use temporal information. DAC (green, combined) improves over single-turn (~0.78 at 2 turns to ~0.83–0.84 for 3–8 turns) but remains notably below pure drift variance across all lengths shown.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot establishes the practical guidance that 4–5 turns are sufficient to capture most of the drift signal, with diminishing returns beyond that. The flat single-turn curve confirms that additional turns only help methods that leverage temporal information. The saturation behavior is consistent across both simple (Node 1ca0: saturates ~0.94 at 4 turns) and sophisticated (Node eba: saturates ~0.99 at 5 turns) simulation setups.

List_of_included_plots.description: AUROC vs. number of turns (Synthetic QA): Both multi-turn signals substantially outperform the single-turn baseline (dashed line at AUROC≈0.823), supporting the core hypothesis that within-conversation dynamics contain strong hallucination signal. The drift/variance-only metric rises sharply with more turns: ~0.85 at 2 turns → ~0.94 at 3 → ~0.97 at 4 → ~0.99 by 5 turns, then saturates near 1.0 through 8 turns. DAC (combining single-turn confidence with drift) starts high (~0.90 at 2 turns), improves to ~0.92–0.95 by 3–4 turns, peaks around ~0.965 at 5 turns, then slightly declines/plateaus (~0.958–0.96) for 6–8 turns.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot from Node eba (more sophisticated simulation with sycophantic pressure) shows a steeper improvement curve for drift variance than Node 1ca0, reaching near-perfect AUROC by 5 turns. Notably, DAC peaks around 5 turns and then slightly declines, while drift variance continues to saturate at near-perfect performance. This suggests that for more complex simulations, the optimal number of turns for DAC may differ from drift variance alone, and that longer conversations can introduce noise into the combined signal.

List_of_included_plots.description: Distribution of confidence drift by hallucination label (Synthetic QA): Correct examples (blue) are sharply concentrated near zero drift variance (very narrow mass around ~0–0.002), while hallucinated examples (red) have a much broader distribution with a heavy right tail extending to ~0.07. Overlap exists at very low drift, but beyond roughly a few thousandths the density is dominated by hallucinated cases. This strongly supports the core hypothesis that within-conversation confidence variance is a discriminative hallucination signal.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The near-perfect separation in this distribution directly explains the high AUROC values (~0.994) for drift variance. Correct answers cluster essentially at zero variance, while hallucinated answers span a broad range. This near-perfect separability raises the important concern that synthetic data generation may have directly encoded drift into labels, and the result should be validated on real LLM data where correct answers may also show some drift due to context changes, paraphrasing, or user pressure.

List_of_included_plots.description: Confidence trajectories across turns (hallucinated vs correct): Hallucinated trajectories (red) show visibly higher variance across turns and across examples, with many lines swinging widely (some dips near ~0.1 and peaks near ~1.0). The mean hallucinated confidence stays around ~0.62–0.67 with a slight bump mid-conversation, indicating hallucinations are not simply 'low confidence'; rather, they are unstable. Correct trajectories (blue) are much tighter, mostly high confidence (~0.8–1.0) with a relatively flat mean around ~0.83–0.85.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This trajectory visualization is the most direct qualitative evidence for the confidence drift hypothesis. It shows that the key discriminating feature is temporal instability (variance), not absolute confidence level — hallucinated claims can show high confidence at some turns and low confidence at others, while correct claims maintain stable high confidence. The mean confidence difference (~0.62-0.67 for hallucinated vs ~0.83-0.85 for correct) is smaller than the variance difference, explaining why drift variance substantially outperforms single-turn confidence as a detection signal.

List_of_included_plots.description: Feature analysis & prediction distributions (Synthetic QA): The scatter of single-turn confidence vs drift variance shows strong class separation primarily along the drift axis: correct points cluster at low drift (~0–0.003) across a range of relatively high single-turn confidences (~0.65–0.95), while hallucinated points occupy much higher drift (~0.003–0.07) and also span a wide range of single-turn confidence (~0.4–0.85). This indicates drift is the dominant feature and single-turn confidence is weakly informative.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The 2D feature scatter confirms that drift variance is the dominant discriminative feature, with nearly perfect vertical separation along the drift axis. Single-turn confidence provides minimal additional separation, as hallucinated and correct examples overlap substantially along the confidence axis. This explains why logistic regression DAC only marginally improves over drift variance alone (0.998 vs 0.994): the logistic regression primarily learns to threshold on drift variance. The Neural DAC prediction histogram showing overlap in the mid-probability region (~0.2-0.5) explains why the neural model underperforms — it fails to exploit the near-perfect linear separability that drift variance provides.

List_of_included_plots.description: Confidence heatmaps (correct vs hallucinated): The correct-answer heatmap is uniformly high-confidence (mostly green, ~0.7–0.95) across turns with relatively small within-row variation, visually demonstrating low drift and supporting the tight near-zero variance distribution. The hallucinated-answer heatmap shows much more heterogeneous patterns: frequent yellow/orange/red patches interspersed with green, indicating strong turn-to-turn fluctuations and occasional confidence collapses for the same claim.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The heatmap visualization provides a complementary view to the trajectory plots, showing the pattern across many examples simultaneously. The uniform green of correct answers vs. the patchy, heterogeneous pattern of hallucinated answers visually confirms that drift is a systematic property of hallucinations rather than a per-example artifact. The presence of sharp drops at specific turns in hallucinated examples suggests that certain conversational contexts (e.g., challenging turns or later turns with more context) may be particularly effective at revealing hallucination instability.

List_of_included_plots.description: ROC curves align with the reported AUROCs: Drift Variance (blue) dominates, hugging the top-left region; DAC (green) is intermediate; Single-turn (red) is weakest but still above random. The early part of the drift ROC indicates strong performance at low false-positive rates (steep initial rise), which is practically important for safety settings where you want to flag hallucinations without over-flagging correct answers.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The ROC curves from Node 1ca0 (simpler simulation) show that drift variance achieves high TPR at very low FPR, which is the practically important operating regime for safety-critical hallucination detection. The hand-combined DAC falls between single-turn and drift, confirming that naive fusion degrades the drift signal. The steep initial rise of the drift ROC curve is particularly important: it means a low-threshold detector based on drift variance can catch a large fraction of hallucinations with very few false positives.

Key_numerical_results.description: Drift Variance AUROC in Node 1ca0 (simpler simulation, hand-combined DAC)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Establishes that even with a simple Gaussian noise simulation, confidence drift variance achieves strong hallucination detection (0.94 AUROC). This is the lower bound on drift AUROC across nodes, suggesting robustness of the signal across different simulation complexities.
The single-turn baseline in the simpler simulation. The gap of ~0.19 AUROC vs drift variance (0.9399) quantifies the benefit of temporal information in the simplest experimental setting.

Hand-combined DAC falls between single-turn (0.7466) and drift variance (0.9399), demonstrating that naive linear combination without learned weights can dilute the stronger drift signal. This motivates using learned fusion (logistic regression) instead.

Key_numerical_results.description: Drift Variance AUROC in Node eba798 (sophisticated simulation with sycophantic pressure)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Near-perfect drift AUROC in the more sophisticated simulation. The jump from 0.9399 (Node 1ca0) to 0.9991 reflects that the sycophantic pressure component creates stronger, more discriminative drift patterns. However, this near-ceiling result also raises concerns about synthetic data encoding drift too directly into labels.

Key_numerical_results.description: Single-Turn Confidence AUROC in Node eba798
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Single-turn baseline is stronger in this simulation (0.8227 vs 0.7466 in Node 1ca0), likely because the sycophantic pressure component also affects mean confidence levels. The drift vs single-turn gap remains large (~0.177 AUROC).

Key_numerical_results.description: DAC AUROC in Node eba798 (hand/weighted combination)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: DAC still underperforms drift variance alone (0.9991) in this node, consistent with Node 1ca0's finding that combination hurts when drift is near-perfect. The DAC improvement over single-turn (0.1367 AUROC) is meaningful but suboptimal.

Key_numerical_results.description: Drift Variance AUROC in Nodes afabe/7401/153 (intermediate simulation, logistic regression DAC)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Consistent across three independently run nodes with identical results, confirming reproducibility. Near-perfect separation, with correct claims concentrated near zero variance and hallucinated claims showing broad distribution with heavy right tail.

Key_numerical_results.description: Single-Turn Confidence AUROC in Nodes afabe/7401/153
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Consistent single-turn baseline across three nodes. The ~0.145 AUROC gap vs drift variance (0.9944) confirms the benefit of temporal information in this intermediate simulation setting.

Key_numerical_results.description: DAC (Logistic Regression) AUROC in Nodes afabe/7401/153
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Logistic regression DAC marginally outperforms drift variance alone (0.9980 vs 0.9944), demonstrating that learned linear fusion preserves the drift signal while extracting small complementary information from single-turn confidence. This contrasts with hand-combined DAC (Nodes 1ca0, eba) which consistently hurt performance, establishing that fusion method matters critically.

Key_numerical_results.description: Neural DAC AUROC in Nodes afabe/7401/153
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Neural DAC substantially underperforms both logistic regression DAC (0.9980) and drift variance alone (0.9944), despite using the same features. This suggests the feature space is nearly linearly separable and a neural network adds variance without improving discrimination. It also suggests the neural model may be miscalibrated or over-parameterized for this task, motivating the use of simpler models for near-linearly-separable hallucination detection settings.

Key_numerical_results.description: Mean Drift Score (Correct answers) in Node 1ca0
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Correct answers show very low mean drift variance (~0.0037), nearly 8x lower than hallucinated answers (0.0294). This quantifies the effect size of the drift phenomenon in the simplest simulation.

Key_numerical_results.description: Mean Drift Score (Hallucinated answers) in Node 1ca0
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Hallucinated answers show ~8x higher mean drift variance than correct answers, providing a strong signal-to-noise ratio for detection. Combined with the distribution plots showing near-zero correct variance, this confirms the discriminative power of the drift metric.

Key_numerical_results.description: Mean Drift Score (Correct answers) in Node eba798
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Even lower correct drift in the sophisticated simulation (~0.0021 vs 0.0037 in Node 1ca0), suggesting the sycophantic pressure component primarily affects hallucinated answers while correct answers remain stable.

Key_numerical_results.description: Mean Drift Score (Hallucinated answers) in Node eba798
Key_numerical_results.analysis: Hallucinated answers show ~15.6x higher mean drift than correct answers in the sophisticated simulation (0.0328/0.0021), a larger ratio than in Node 1ca0 (7.9x), explaining the near-perfect AUROC. The sycophantic pressure component amplifies drift for hallucinated claims specifically.
